name: Shimmercat
desc: A sleek, low-slung quadruped roughly the size of a large domestic cat. Bioluminescent patches along the spine pulse faintly in cool blues and greens, particularly when hunting or distressed. Its fur is fine and dark, absorbing ambient light.
narration: [sardonic]You won't see it coming. You'll feel it. [pause]A line of cold along the back of your neck. [pause]A breath that wasn't yours. [pause]Just enough to mark the moment your luck ran out. [pause]The new arrivals call it the Shimmercat. The older hands call it the Ghost-cat. Same animal. Both names accurate. [pause]No bigger than the housecat back home. That part is the joke. The rest is that it survived a world that ate its competition for breakfast. You're not on its menu. You're just on its land. [pause]It only wants you to know you weren't invited.

name: Clackers
desc: A two-meter-long, semi-aquatic predator native to the Mairee Delta. Protected by a blunt carapace and propelled by paddle-shaped limbs, the Clacker is defined by a colossal beak capable of crushing giant shellfish and shearing through metal building foundations. They hunt in packs and are a primary threat to water extraction outposts, though they can be repelled by high-frequency sonic emitters..
narration: A wet clatter — armor moving like a barge through mud. They aren't hunting you. They're patrolling. The Delta is theirs. The water at the edge of it is also theirs. The metal pylons you sank into the soft ground to draw it out — those are theirs too, now. The beak. Black. Curved. Designed for shellfish but indifferent to the difference between a shell and a steel foundation. A high-frequency emitter will scare them off. Most of the time.

name: Dartswarm
desc: A swarm of hand-sized, dragonfly-like insects native to the Mairee Delta. Defined by saw-edged razor wings and a hard chitinous shell, the Dartswarm is a relentless predator that attacks in massive, high-speed clusters. They are capable of stripping flesh in seconds. Their attacks are venomous and accompanied by a high-pitched shrieking.
narration: They come as a sound first. A high keening you mistake for wind, then for tinnitus. Before you know it they are actually inside your skull. Wings like razorblades. They strip what they close in on. If that didn't kill you, perhaps the venom will.

name: Vurnshaft
desc: A Vurnshaft is a living subterranean tunnel system carved by the Talpochelys serpentina. It eats and shits its way through the soil. The shaft is characterized by rubbery, pulsing walls and sugarcane-blue bioluminescence, the network is deeply integrated into the planet's Myco-Rhizomic System. While they offer a "highway" beneath the hostile surface of Mairee, the tunnels are biologically active, capable of hardening to stone or liquefying in response to stimuli, and contain massive geothermal hatcheries known as Vurn nodes.
narration: A Vurnshaft is a living subterranean tunnel system carved by the Talpochelys serpentina. It eats and shits its way through the soil. You'll be told these tunnels are a shortcut. You'll want to believe it — mostly because anything on the surface will be trying very hard to kill you. The Vurnshaft is rubbery underfoot. Warm. It pulses with a blue light that feels like it knows you're there. The walls breathe. Sometimes they harden to stone in the time it takes you to turn around. It is a tunnel only in the sense that an esophagus is a tunnel.

name: Vurn (Talpochelys serpentina)
desc: A massive subterranean predator scientifically classified as Talpochelys serpentina. This "part turtle, part serpent" creature is defined by its obsidian-tipped claws and a beak that splits open to reveal a whip-like sensory tongue. The Vurn is responsible for creating the vast network of vurnshafts (tunnels) beneath the Mairee Delta. It is an apex threat capable of destroying armored shuttles and is deeply integrated into the planet's living fungal network.,
narration: The taxonomists named it Talpochelys serpentina because they had to call it something. Part turtle. Part serpent. Neither word adequate. It moves through soil and rock the way you move through water. It hears with its skin. Its beak parts to reveal a tongue that is also a whip and also a sensor array. It made the vurnshafts. It maintains them. It consumes and shits out whatever was unwise enough to enter one — including, on one documented occasions, an armored shuttle.

name: Spore Storms
desc: A psychedelic atmospheric event triggered by the planet's world-spanning fungal network. Spore Storms are defined by shimmering, pearly particulates that create a colorful vortex in the sky. These storms are a major threat to human settlers, causing auditory hallucinations, blurred vision, and technical sensor failure. Survival requires specialized filtration masks to clear the air.
narration: You'll think it's beautiful. You'll be right. That's part of the problem. The sky will go pearly and start to spin — not visibly, just enough that your inner ear knows something has changed before your eyes do. Then the colors. The hum that isn't a hum. A choir you can't quite hear, singing in a register that bypasses your ears and goes straight to the part of you that decides what is real. Filtration masks help. Distance helps more. They aren't weather. They are a thought the planet is having. You happened to be inside it. The Blooms. Fog-heads. By the time you've named it, you've already inhaled the next sentence.

name: Burr-vine
desc: A deceptively delicate, feathery plant native to the moist soils of the Mairee Delta. The Burr-vine is a significant environmental hazard defined by seed pods with microscopic barbs that latch onto clothing and skin. Once attached, the barbs burrow into the flesh, causing intense irritation and lethal infections.
narration: It looks like a Wooly feather. That's the first lie. Run your hand against it the way you would against a fern back home, and you will spend the next six clicks of your life regretting it. The seeds are barbed in the microscopic sense of barbed. They don't catch on your skin. They go into it. The vine releases them as a courtesy — the way a host might offer a small parting gift. The infection comes later. Most settlers learn the cost early. Some learn it late, in a med-tent, while a medic explains what 'systemic' means. Grip-twig, the older hands call it.

desc: A huge, funnel-shaped plant native to the Mairee Delta that resembles an antique gramophone. Capable of emitting sounds ranging from melancholic moans to piercing shrieks, the plant is a significant source of auditory disorientation. It often serves as a biological amplifier for wind and spore clouds, and under the influence of the planet’s consciousness, it can even produce strange, alien music.
narration: The plants on Mairee are louder. Sit with them long enough and you'll start asking the wrong questions. The Gramophone Plant is a funnel a meter across — sometimes two — sitting in deep clearings where the wind has somewhere to go. It moans. It shrieks. It whispers occasionally, on still days, like it's letting you in on something it heard down the line. Shriekers, the survey teams call them. Wind instruments grown by a world that wanted to sound like itself. Some claim it sings. Some claim it whispers. Some claims it sucks your soul from you.

name: Scrudgar
desc: A tusked, blubbery herbivore native to the slow rivers and valleys of the Sickle mountain range. Roughly the size and shape of a mud-caked barrel set on four stout legs. Scrudgars spend most of their days and nights submerged up to their small, beady eyes, lazily munching on submerged roots. At dusk they emerge to bask and grunt — a low, carrying call that echoes for kilometers through the Delta valleys. Despite the impressive tusks, scrudgars are placid and have shown no recorded aggression toward colonists or scitter drones. Among the first Mairee creatures to be widely adopted as a colony mascot; plushie versions are a common nursery fixture at the Freya Homestead.
narration: Mud-caked. Blubbery. Mostly submerged. Tusks it almost never uses. The scrudgar spends its days and nights up to its small beady eyes in a muddy pool, munching on roots and — as best we can tell — thinking about more roots. At dusk they grunt. The sound carries for kilometers down the valleys. On Mairee, almost everything that wants to kill you announces itself differently. The scrudgar's grunt is, mostly, the sound of a creature being content. The crew loves them. Nobody looks too closely at the tusks.
